title: The Few-Shot Formatting Pattern
concept: Few-shot examples must follow a consistent format (e.g. Q: ... A: ...) to teach the model the input-output mapping clearly.

exercises.prompt: When giving an AI multiple examples (few-shot prompting), what helps the AI understand the pattern best?
exercises.explain: Few-shot prompting relies on showing the AI examples of the task. For this to work, the examples must follow a highly consistent format.

cards.title: Keep It Consistent!
cards.body: Have you ever tried to teach someone a game, but changed the rules every round? They would probably get super confused! The same thing happens to AI models when we give them messy, inconsistent examples. Let's learn how to format our prompts so the AI understands us perfectly every time.

cards.title: Few-Shot Formatting
cards.body: The Few-Shot Formatting Pattern means structuring all your examples in the exact same way. By repeating a clear layout, like 'Input: ... Output: ...', you show the AI exactly where the question ends and where the answer begins.

cards.title: The Puppy Training Rule
cards.body: Think of training a dog to sit. If you say 'Sit!' and give a treat, then say 'Please sit down' and give a toy, and then blow a whistle, the dog gets confused. But if you always say 'Sit!' and hand over a treat in the exact same way, the dog learns the pattern instantly. Consistent formatting does the same for AI!

cards.title: Matching Templates
cards.body:
If you want the AI to translate words and give a emoji, format your examples like this:

Word: Apple -> Translation: Manzana 🍎
Word: Dog -> Translation: Perro 🐶
Word: Sun -> Translation: Sol ☀️

Because the format is identical, the AI knows exactly how to answer the next one!

cards.title: Does Format Really Matter?
cards.body: Many people think AI is smart enough to understand examples written in any random format. In reality, if you mix up your formatting (like using 'Q:' in one example and 'Question:' in another), the AI will often get confused and generate messy, unpredictable outputs.

cards.title: Spot the Pattern
cards.body: Look at how structure makes a difference. Keeping labels and symbols identical helps the AI predict the next line of text.

right.text: Input: Run -> Output: Go\nInput: Jump -> Output: Leap\nInput: Fly -> Output: Soar

left.text: Input: Run -> Go\nWord: Jump -> Leap\nActive: Fly -> Output: Soar

exercises.prompt: Which of the following prompts uses the Few-Shot Formatting Pattern correctly to train the AI?

options.text: Q: Happy -> A: Positive \nQ: Sad -> A: Negative \nQ: Angry -> A:
Review: Happy / Sentiment is Positive \nSad: Negative \nQ: Angry ->
Q: Happy -> A: Positive \nInput: Sad -> Output: Negative \nIs angry positive?

exercises.explain: The first option uses a clear, consistent pattern (Q: ... A: ...) for all examples, making it easy for the AI to follow. The other options mix up the labels or don't use any structure.

exercises.prompt: True or False: It is perfectly fine to change the labels from 'Q:' to 'Question:' halfway through your few-shot examples.
exercises.explain: Even if the information is correct, changing the layout or labels (like swapping 'Q:' for 'Question:') confuses the AI's pattern-matching ability.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule about few-shot prompting.
exercises.sentence: To help the AI learn a pattern, every example must use the exact same format.
exercises.explain: Using the exact same format (like matching labels and spacing) for all examples helps the AI recognize the pattern and generate the correct output.

exercises.prompt: An AI is given a pattern of opposites. Which of these shows the AI correctly continuing the Few-Shot Formatting Pattern?

options.text: Input: Up -> Output: Down \nInput: Big -> Output: Small \nInput: Cold -> Output: Water is very cold in winter.
Input: Up -> Output: Down \nInput: Big -> Output: Small \nInput: Cold -> Output: Hot
Input: Up -> Output: Down \nInput: Big -> Output: Small \nWhat is the opposite of Cold? It is hot.

exercises.explain: The AI output is the one that successfully copies the pattern established by the two user examples, converting the input 'Cold' to its opposite 'Hot'.

exercises.prompt: You want to build a tool that extracts movie names from reviews using few-shot prompting. What is the best way to design your examples to ensure the AI outputs exactly what you want?

options.text: Write examples using different labels like 'Question:' and then 'Input:' to keep it interesting.
Create a strict template like 'Input: [text] \nOutput: [label]' and apply it to every single example.
Avoid using labels entirely and just paste random sentences.
Only provide one example and change the format in the final prompt.

exercises.explain: To teach an AI a new format, your few-shot examples must strictly use the exact same template (like Input: / Output:) across all examples. Mixing different styles or leaving out labels will break the pattern and confuse the model.
